sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Blood pressure is a core vital sign that helps assess perfusion, shock, cardiovascular risk, and response to treatment. In prehospital care, the number must always be interpreted in context with the patient's overall presentation, trend over time, and the reliability of the measurement.

sections.title: Procedure - Manual (Auscultatory)
sections.content:
**Equipment**
- Sphygmomanometer and stethoscope
- Appropriately sized cuff

**Procedure**
1. Position the patient and arm as well as conditions allow
2. Apply the cuff to the bare upper arm where practicable
3. Estimate systolic pressure first if needed, then auscultate over the brachial artery
4. Deflate slowly and record systolic and diastolic values
5. Use palpatory or automated methods when noise, movement, or urgency make auscultation impractical
6. Repeat abnormal readings when clinically appropriate and trend over time

sections.title: Interpretation
sections.content:
- Hypotension should always be interpreted alongside pulse, skin signs, conscious state, mechanism, and likely pathology
- A single normal BP does not exclude evolving shock
- Severe hypertension matters most when there is evidence of acute end-organ dysfunction rather than the number alone
- Pulse pressure and BP trend may be clinically useful, especially in trauma, sepsis, and cardiac presentations
- In children, age-appropriate thresholds and the overall clinical picture are essential

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Use the correct cuff size because undersized cuffs over-read and oversized cuffs under-read
- Repeat abnormal readings and trend them over time rather than relying on one measurement
- Avoid affected limbs where possible, such as major injury, vascular access, dialysis fistula, or post-mastectomy side
- In noisy or moving environments, palpatory or automated readings may be more practical, but reliability must still be judged clinically
- Document site, method, patient position, and any factors that may have affected the reading
